questions.title: What does Hajj al-Qiran mean?
questions.answer: Performing Umrah and Hajj together without coming out of ihram
questions.explanation:
"Hajj al-Qiran" refers to a type of Hajj pilgrimage where a pilgrim combines the rites of Umrah and Hajj in one consecration of ihram.

In this type of Hajj, the pilgrim assumes the state of ihram for both Umrah and Hajj together without coming out of it until the completion of the Hajj rituals.


It's one of the three types of Hajj, the others being

  1. "Hajj al-Ifrad"
  2. "Hajj al-Tamattu"
  3. "Hajj al-Qiran"

questions.title: Animals are routinely sacrificed on Eid-ul-Adha in memory of the sacrifice of?
questions.explanation:
• Animals are routinely sacrificed on Eid ul Adha in memory of the sacrifice of Prophet Abraham and Prophet Ishmael.
  • Prophet Abraham was tested by God and asked to sacrifice his son, Prophet Ishmael, as a demonstration of his faith.
  • However, just as Abraham was about to carry out the sacrifice, God provided a ram to be sacrificed instead.

• "Mabrood" is an Arabic term that means "blessed" or "fortunate."

questions.explanation: • "Hadi" in the context of Hajj refers to the sacrificial animal that pilgrims offer during the ritual of Qurbani (sacrifice) as part of the Hajj pilgrimage.

questions.title: Umrah can be performed at any time throughout the year except?
questions.explanation: • Muslims can conduct Umrah at any time of the year, except during the Hajj season. Hajj is the annual pilgrimage to Mecca that takes place in the Islamic month of Dhu al-Hijjah.

questions.title: What is the difference between Hajj and Umrah?
questions.answer: Hajj is offered between 7th to 13th Zul-Hajj whereas Umrah can be performed any time
questions.explanation: • Umrah can be performed at any time during the Islamic calendar, unlike Hajj which can only be performed during the month of Dhul Hijjah.

questions.title: Who rebuilt Kaaba 4500 years ago?
questions.explanation:
• The Kaaba was originally built by Prophet Ibrahim (Abraham) and his son Ismael around 4,500 years ago.
  • They are believed to have been commanded by Allah to construct the Kaaba as a place of worship.

questions.title: How many companions accompanied Prophet (PBUH) at the last Hajj?
questions.explanation: • At the Prophet Muhammad's (PBUH) last Hajj, known as the Farewell Pilgrimage, there were approximately 120,000 companions present.

questions.title: The most of the bindings of Hajj on the pilgrim are released on____after Rami.
questions.explanation: • The most of the bindings of Hajj on the pilgrim are released on the 10th day of Dhu al-Hijjah, which is after the ritual of Rami (stoning of the devil). This day is known as Eid al-Adha.

questions.title: Tawaf-e-Ziyarat is performed on?
questions.explanation:
• Tawaf al-Ziyarah is a farz.
  • It can be performed any time, day or night, from 10 Zil Hijjah to the sunset of 12 Zil Hijjah.

questions.title: When Tawaf-e-Sadr is performed?
questions.explanation:
• Tawaf al-Wida (the Farewell Tawaf), also known as Tawaf al-Sadr (Tawaf of Leaving).
  • This is performed by Hajj pilgrims just before leaving Makkah after completing the Hajj.

questions.title: Which pillar of Islam is an exhibition of brotherhood, equality punctuality and discipline?
questions.explanation: • The pilgrimage of Hajj, which is one of the Five Pillars of Islam, serves as an exhibition of brotherhood, equality, punctuality, and discipline.

questions.explanation: • Tawaf-e-Ziyarat or Tawaf-e-Ifadah is also known as Tawaf-e-Rukn.

questions.explanation: • The small piece of land between Rukn-e-Islam (the Black Stone) and Rukn-e-Yamani (the Yemeni Corner) is known as the "Hatim" or "Hijr Isma'il."

questions.title: After which rite of Hajj, the Talbiyah is to be stopped?
questions.explanation: • After the ritual of throwing pebbles at the Jamarat (the three pillars representing Satan) on the 10th day of Dhu al-Hijjah, pilgrims participating in Hajj are instructed to stop reciting the Talbiyah. This act is known as "Ramy al-Jamarat".

questions.explanation: • Hajj is Farz (obligatory) once in a lifetime for a Muslim who is capable to do so.

questions.title: Which rite of Hajj is celebrated throughout the world by Muslims?
questions.explanation:
• The rite of Hajj celebrated throughout the world by Muslims is the Eid al-Adha, also known as the Festival of Sacrifice.
  • This celebration takes place on the 10th day of Dhu al-Hijjah.

questions.title: What is/are the condition(s) to perform Hajj for a Muslim?
questions.explanation:
The conditions to perform Hajj for a Muslim include:

  • Islam: The individual must be a Muslim.
  • Maturity: The person should be mentally and physically mature.
  • Financial Capability: The individual must have the financial means to undertake the journey without causing hardship to themselves or their dependents.
  • Physical Capability: The person should be physically capable of performing the pilgrimage, and having the provisions for travel is part of this readiness.
  • Freedom: The individual should be free, both physically and legally, to undertake the journey.

questions.title: Where did Hazrat Hawa A.S and Hazrat Adam A.S reunite after years of wanderings?
questions.explanation:
• Hazrat Hawwa (Eve) and Hazrat Adam (Adam) reunited at the plain of Arafat after years of wanderings.
  • The story symbolizes their reconciliation and repentance after being separated due to their initial dwelling in Paradise and subsequent descent to Earth.

questions.title: Which is the holiest and most sacred spot on the surface of the earth for Muslims?
questions.explanation: • Mecca is considered the holiest city in Islam, as it is home to Islam's holiest site Kaaba in Al-Masjid Al-Ḥaram (The Sacred Mosque).

questions.title: In the first ever Hajj in Islam (in 9 AH) it’s Ameer was?
questions.explanation: • Muslim tradition ascribes the first Hajj caravan to the lifetime of the Islamic Prophet Muhammad SAW, who in 630 (AH 9) instructed Ameer Hazrat Abu Bakr to lead 300 pilgrims from Medina to Mecca.

questions.title: Which Ibadat is the best demonstration of unity, confederation and equality of the Islamic world?
questions.explanation:
• Hajj is considered the best demonstration of unity, confederation, and equality in the Islamic world.
  • During Hajj, Muslims from diverse backgrounds, cultures, and nations gather in Makkah to perform the pilgrimage rituals together, emphasizing the universal brotherhood and equality among believers.

Pilgrims spend the night at Muzdalifah on the 9th and 10th days of Dhu al-Hijjah.

Engaging in the ritual of Wuquf, which involves standing in prayer, supplication, and collecting pebbles for the symbolic stoning of the Jamarat.

questions.title: Which place is called Miqat for pilgrims coming from Pakistan?
questions.explanation:
Meeqat for the people of PAKISTAN is Yalamlam
Zul Halifa for Madina people 
***

Typically there are 5 Miqats

1. Yalamlam Miqat: This is the Miqat for the people of Yemen, India, Pakistan, China, South Africa, Nigeria, Japan.

2. Al-Juhfah: This Miqat is also known as Rabigh. This is the Miqat for the people of North America, North Africa, Jordan, Lebanon, Europe, Turkey, Syria, Egypt, Algeria, Sudan.

3. Dhat Irq: This is the Miqat for the people of Iraq, Iran, China, and Russia.

4. Qarn Al-Manazil (Miqat Taif): This Miqat is also known as Al Sail Al Kabir. This is the Miqat for the people of U.A.E, Oman, Pakistan, Australia, Malaysia, Singapore, Najd, Riyadh, Dammam, and Khobar.

5. Dhul Hulayfah: This Miqat is also known as Abyar Ali. This Miqat is for the people of Medina.

questions.title: What is the Qur’anic argument on the Life Hereafter? What are its impacts on the individual and collective life of a human being?
questions.explanation:
What is the Qur’anic argument on the Life Hereafter? What are its impacts on the individual and collective life of a human being? (20 marks)

  1. Purpose and Accountability: The Qur'an states that life is a test and that humans are created to worship and obey God. Accountability in the Hereafter ensures every action is rewarded or punished.

    • "Do you think that We created you in play (without any purpose)?" (Qur'an 23:115).

  2. Resurrection and Divine Justice: The Qur'an highlights resurrection as a demonstration of God's power, ensuring ultimate justice.

    • "And to Allah belongs the Hereafter and the former (life)" (Qur'an 53:25).
    • "The Day when everyone will be paid in full for what they have earned" (Qur'an 3:185).

  3. Natural Signs as Evidence: The Qur'an frequently points to signs in nature (e.g., plants revived from dead soil) as evidence of resurrection.

    • "Look at the earth, how We bring life to it after its death" (Qur'an 30:19).
  1. Moral Accountability: Awareness of judgment day fosters honesty, integrity, and a strong moral compass.
  2. Encouragement of Good Deeds: Promotes acts of charity, kindness, and patience due to the promise of reward.
  3. Inner Peace: Belief in divine justice brings comfort in facing life's hardships and injustices.
  1. Promotion of Justice and Social Welfare: Encourages fair dealings and the establishment of justice in society.
  2. Strengthening of Social Bonds: Compassion and charity are encouraged, creating trust and support within communities.
  3. Discouragement of Social Vices: Reduces crimes, corruption, and exploitation by instilling fear of accountability.

In essence, belief in the Hereafter shapes ethical, social, and spiritual behaviors, guiding both individuals and communities toward a just and compassionate society
